error_type: Mixing 把 and 被 in one clause
why_english_speakers: English has no separate disposal marker, so learners grab both 把 and 被 to signal 'something was done to the object', not realising the two constructions are mutually exclusive.
correct: 书被他借走了。 / 她把书借给了他。
note: 把 = subject acts on object; 被 = subject is acted upon. Choose one based on which noun is the topic.

error_type: Treating 了 as a general past-tense marker
why_english_speakers: English marks all past actions with '-ed', so learners attach 了 to every past verb, including habitual and stative ones where Chinese uses no aspect marker.
incorrect: 小时候我每天喝了牛奶。
note: 了 marks completion of a specific event, not past tense. Habitual past takes no 了.

why_english_speakers: English says 'three books' with no classifier, so learners go straight from number to noun and drop the measure word.
note: Number/demonstrative + Measure Word + Noun is obligatory in Chinese.

error_type: Overusing 个 as a default measure word
why_english_speakers: Knowing a measure word is needed but not which one, learners default to 个 for every noun.
note: 个 is general but many nouns need a specific measure word (本 books, 张 flat items, 只 animals).

error_type: Using 很/非常 in a 比 comparison
why_english_speakers: English 'much taller / very much taller' pushes learners to add an intensifier, but 比 sentences reject 很/非常.
correct: 他比我高。 / 他比我高得多。
note: For degree in a 比 sentence use 更, or a quantity like 一点儿 / 得多.

error_type: English word order for time and place
why_english_speakers: English places time and place after the verb ('I go to China next year'), so learners keep that order in Chinese.
note: Chinese: Subject + Time + Place + Verb + Object.

why_english_speakers: 的, 得, and 地 are homophones (all 'de') with no spoken distinction, so learners write the most familiar 的 everywhere.
note: 的 (noun modifier), 地 (adverb + verb), 得 (verb + degree complement).

error_type: Dropping the second half of a paired conjunction
why_english_speakers: English uses only one connector ('although', 'because'), so learners omit the Chinese partner (但是, 所以).
correct: 虽然很贵，但是我还是买了。
note: Chinese commonly keeps both: 虽然……但是, 因为……所以, 不但……而且.

error_type: Using 了 where experiential 过 is meant
why_english_speakers: English present-perfect 'I have been to...' maps ambiguously; learners reach for 了 instead of the experiential 过.
incorrect: 我去了法国，很漂亮。（meaning: I have been to France）
note: 过 = have ever experienced; 了 = a specific completed event.

error_type: 把 sentence with a bare verb
why_english_speakers: Learners front the object with 把 but forget that the verb needs a result/complement/了 to show disposal.
note: 把 + Object + Verb + (complement/了/result). A bare verb is ungrammatical.

error_type: Using 能 for a learned skill
why_english_speakers: English 'can' covers ability, capability, and permission, so learners pick 能 for everything, including learned skills that need 会.
note: 会 = acquired skill; 能 = capability/possibility right now; 可以 = permission.

why_english_speakers: English says 'I studied for two hours' with duration late but treats it like an adverb; learners front it like a time-when phrase.
note: Duration (how long) goes AFTER the verb; repeat the verb if there is an object.

error_type: Using 或者 in a choice question
why_english_speakers: English uses 'or' for both statements and questions, so learners use 或者 in questions where 还是 is required.
note: 还是 = 'or' in choice questions; 或者 = 'or' in statements.

error_type: Negating a completed/resultative verb with 不
why_english_speakers: Learners default to 不 for all negation, but completed actions and results are negated with 没(有).
note: 没 negates completion/result and past events; 不 negates habits, willingness, and present/future.

error_type: Inserting 是 before a predicate adjective
why_english_speakers: English needs 'to be' before adjectives ('she is tall'), so learners add 是 where Chinese uses 很 or nothing.
note: Adjectives are predicates on their own; use 很 (often bleached) not 是.

error_type: Using 吗 for a 'what about you' follow-up
why_english_speakers: Learners know 吗 makes questions and apply it to the elliptical 'and you?' where Chinese needs 呢.
note: 呢 asks 'what about X?' when the topic is already set; 吗 makes a yes/no question.
